slides.title: Every Innovation Cuts Both Ways
slides.subtitle: The same app that saves your commute can wreck a neighborhood — why every computing innovation helps and harms at once
slides.footerLeft: AP Computer Science Principles · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2023)
slides.script: Welcome to Big Idea 5, the Impact of Computing. Topic 5.1 asks the unit's founding question: when people build computing innovations, what do those innovations do back to people? We spend two days here. Today builds the effect-analysis toolkit — how effects appear, why they surprise their makers, and the exam's favorite move: one single effect that is beneficial and harmful at the same time. Tomorrow follows innovations that escaped their blueprints entirely, and launches Innovation Investigation #3.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Explain how a single effect of a computing innovation can be viewed as both beneficial and harmful (LO IOC-1.A)
Explain why effects go unanticipated, and how computing advances fuel creativity in other fields (LO IOC-1.A)

slides.script: Two targets today, both inside LO IOC-1.A. First, the dual-view skill: showing how one effect — not a good effect plus a separate bad one — can be judged beneficial by one person and harmful by another, or both by the same person. Second, the supporting facts around it: innovations are created by people, tasks reorganize around them, effects arrive unannounced, and the ripples reach fields far outside computing. Every free-response prompt in this unit leans on today's vocabulary.

slides.prompt: Half the school opens the same short-video app before first period. Think of one specific user — maybe you.
slides.subprompt: List 3 ways the app makes that ONE user's life better and 3 ways it makes the SAME user's life worse. Star anything that lands on both lists.
slides.directions: Be ready to share your thinking.
slides.script: Five minutes, on your own. One short-video app, one user, two lists: three ways it improves that user's life, three ways it damages the same user's life. The interesting instruction is the star: mark anything that shows up on both lists — the endless feed that entertains AND eats the evening, the audience that encourages AND judges. Keep the lists; by the end of class you will know that the starred items are exactly what the College Board calls a single effect viewed as both beneficial and harmful.

slides.heading: What You'll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Day 1 Guided Notes have 3 sections that match today's lesson. Green = CB required.

sections.title: How Innovations Change Us
sections.description: People create innovations, tasks reorganize, and creativity spreads to other fields (EK IOC-1.A.1–A.2, A.5).

sections.title: One Effect, Two Verdicts
sections.description: Unanticipated effects, and single effects that help and harm at once (EK IOC-1.A.3–A.4).

sections.title: Deep Dive: The Collingridge Dilemma
sections.description: Why the moment you can steer a technology is the moment you can't yet see where it's going.

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. Absent or lost your packet? A digital copy lives on the Topic 5.1 Guided Notes page at apcsexamprep.com.
slides.script: Here's the map for today's Guided Notes — two green CB-required sections, how innovations change us and the dual-verdict effect, plus a blue deep-dive on the Collingridge dilemma for the enrichment track. Keep the packet open and fill it in as we go; if you were absent or lost the packet, a digital copy lives on the Topic 5.1 Guided Notes page at apcsexamprep.com.

slides.label: How Innovations Change Us
slides.kicker: People, Tasks, and Ripple Effects · Learning Objective IOC-1.A
slides.script: Section one. Before judging any effect, three background facts have to be on the table: innovations are made by people, people then remake their routines around the innovations, and the ripples travel into fields that have nothing to do with computing. The College Board states all three as Essential Knowledge, and exam items quietly assume them.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO IOC-1.A)

terms.definition: An innovation — physical, software, or concept — that includes a program as an integral part of its function; computing innovations are created by people (EK IOC-1.A.1).
A change an innovation causes in people's lives, often in how tasks get done — the way people complete tasks changes to incorporate new innovations (EK IOC-1.A.2).
An effect experienced as helpful by a person or group; the same effect can be judged beneficial by one viewer and harmful by another (EK IOC-1.A.4).
An effect experienced as damaging; whether an effect counts as harmful depends on who is judging it and what they have at stake (EK IOC-1.A.4).
An effect the innovation's makers did not predict — not every effect of a computing innovation is anticipated in advance (EK IOC-1.A.3).

slides.script: Five terms carry the day. Computing innovation is the anchor from Topic 1.1: a program integral to the function, and — the part this unit adds — created by people, which means every impact traces back to decisions. Effect is the unit's working word: a change in how people live, usually visible as a change in how a task gets done. Beneficial and harmful are deliberately defined from the viewer's seat, not the technology's spec sheet — the same effect can wear both labels. And unanticipated effect is the CED's honest admission that surprises are guaranteed. Students write every definition in the packet; that is the standing rule for every vocabulary section.

slides.heading: People Build, Life Reorders
bullets: People create computing innovations — every app, device, and algorithm traces back to decisions by its makers about what to build and for whom (EK IOC-1.A.1).
The way people complete tasks often changes to incorporate new innovations — finding directions, studying, paying, and meeting people have all been redesigned around software (EK IOC-1.A.2).
Not every effect of an innovation is anticipated in advance — real users, at real scale, create consequences nobody wrote down (EK IOC-1.A.3).
The effects keep unfolding for years: an innovation's story is written by its users at least as much as by its makers.
slides.script: Three Essential Knowledge statements in one arc. Innovations start with people — which matters on the exam: nothing about a tool's impact is fate; it flows from what someone chose to build. Then adoption flips the arrow: once the innovation exists, people rebuild their routines around it — think of how completely 'getting somewhere' or 'studying for a test' changed shape in a decade. And the third statement is the humble one: not every effect is anticipated in advance. Real users at real scale generate situations the design meeting never imagined, which is why an innovation's story keeps being written long after launch.

capture.q: Innovations don't appear on their own: ______ decide what gets built.
capture.a: human beings — a maker's priorities end up baked into the product

capture.q: When a new tool spreads, everyday tasks ______.
capture.a: bend to fit it — the former routine quietly fades away
live audiences at full size behave in ways no planning meeting covered

slides.heading: Computing Sparks Other Fields
slides.subheading: Advances in computing have generated and increased creativity in other fields (EK IOC-1.A.5).

items.title: Medicine
items.summary: Imaging software, protein-folding models, and surgical simulation — treatments exist today that biology alone never produced.

items.title: Engineering & the arts
items.summary: Bridges are stress-tested in simulation before a bolt is made; films, music, and games are now composed inside software.

items.title: Communications
items.summary: Instant translation, video calls across oceans, publishing open to anyone — whole careers now live on these tools.

slides.script: The CED names four fields where computing raised the creative ceiling: medicine, engineering, communications, and the arts. The point to model out loud is the direction of the claim — this is computing acting on OTHER fields, not computing improving itself. A surgeon rehearsing on a 3D model of the actual patient's heart, an engineer breaking a bridge a thousand times in simulation, a musician producing an album in a bedroom: none of these people are computer scientists, and all of them work in ways computing made possible. Tomorrow this idea returns with a twist — sometimes the boost to another field was never planned at all.

slides.label: One Effect, Two Verdicts
slides.kicker: Beneficial, Harmful, or Both · Learning Objective IOC-1.A
slides.script: Section two, the heart of the learning objective. Look back at the starred items from the bell ringer — the entries that landed on both the helps list and the harms list. The College Board's claim is precise: a single effect can be viewed as both beneficial and harmful, by different people or even by the same person. The skill is keeping it singular — one effect, two judgments.

slides.heading: One Effect, Two Verdicts
slides.subheading: The SAME effect, judged from two seats (EK IOC-1.A.4) — not a good effect plus a bad one.

left.title: Judged beneficial
left.definition: The effect, as experienced by the people it serves
examples: Feed tuned to your taste — entertainment that never runs dry
App reroutes you around traffic — minutes back every day
Self-checkout: no lines, faster shopping trips

right.title: Judged harmful
right.definition: The very same effect, as experienced by the people it costs
examples: Same tuning makes the app hard to close — sleep and homework pay
Same rerouting floods quiet streets with cut-through traffic
Same automation ends the cashier shift a family relied on

slides.footer: AP TIP: The CED's phrase is one effect “viewed as both beneficial and harmful by different people, or even by the same person.” Keep it singular.
slides.script: Read this slide across, not down — each row is ONE effect wearing two verdicts. The personalized feed that never runs out of clips is the same mechanism that makes 2 a.m. arrive early; nothing changes in the code between the two judgments, only the seat you view it from. The rerouting row previews tomorrow's exercise: drivers gain the exact minutes that Maple Street residents lose in noise and traffic. And notice the third row can split one person in half — a shopper who loves self-checkout may be the parent of the cashier it replaced. That is EK IOC-1.A.4's full claim: different people, or even the same person.

capture.q: To show a dual-view effect, point to ______ and two different judgments of it.
capture.a: one and the same outcome — an identical change observed by opposing sides

capture.q: Whether an effect counts as help or harm depends on ______.
capture.a: whose seat you occupy — a person's position and stakes, not the code

capture.q: A single person can hold both views at once when ______.
capture.a: the outcome serves one of their goals while undermining another

slides.heading: “A Good Innovation Has Only Good Effects”
slides.misconception: If an innovation is well designed and genuinely useful, its effects will be positive; harmful effects only come from badly built or malicious technology.
slides.whyItHappens: Marketing tells only success stories, and we judge tools by our own experience — if it helps me, it feels like 'good technology.'
slides.correction: Effects depend on who is affected and how the innovation is used — no matter how well it is built. Not every effect is anticipated in advance (EK IOC-1.A.3), and a single effect can be viewed as beneficial AND harmful by different people, or the same person (EK IOC-1.A.4).
slides.example: The recommendation feed is 'great suggestions' to a viewer at 7 p.m. and 'the reason I'm exhausted' to the SAME viewer at 2 a.m. — one effect, two verdicts, zero bugs involved.
slides.script: Here's the trap this topic exists to remove. Students arrive believing quality determines impact: good engineering produces good effects, harm means someone built it badly or maliciously. The CED says otherwise on both counts. Surprises are structural — not every effect is anticipated in advance — and verdicts are seat-dependent — the identical effect can be a blessing in one life and a cost in another, or both within one person on the same day. The feed example lands well: nothing malfunctions between 7 p.m. and 2 a.m. except the user's own interests. Students explain in the packet why the myth fails; the strongest explanations use the word 'who.'

slides.heading: Effect Triage: The Short-Video App
slides.context: Four documented effects of the bell-ringer app. For each: beneficial, harmful, or BOTH — and was it anticipated in advance?
rows: A teen's science clips reach millions and land a scholarship
Beneficial — reach no studio would ever give an unknown
Yes — the app was built to make sharing easy
The feed keeps users watching for hours
BOTH — fans call it entertainment; the same viewer calls it a time drain at 2 a.m.
Partly — engagement was the goal; the lost-sleep backlash was not
Viral 'challenges' push risky stunts into schools
Harmful — injuries and discipline cases from copycat behavior
No — no design meeting listed 'stunt epidemics'
Local businesses hire students as short-video managers
Beneficial — a job category that did not exist five years ago
No — an economic ripple nobody planned
slides.script: Four real-shaped effects of the bell-ringer app, two rulings each. Row one is the clean case: beneficial, and fully anticipated — sharing widely is what the app is for. Row two is the exam's favorite: the engaging feed is a single effect that earns BOTH verdicts, sometimes from the same viewer, and only half of it was anticipated — engagement was engineered, the backlash was not. Row three is pure unanticipated harm, and row four is the quiet one students miss: an unplanned effect can be a whole new job market. The diagnosis discipline: rule on the effect and on the anticipation separately — they are independent axes.

slides.heading: Deep Dive: The Collingridge Dilemma
bullets: When a technology is NEW, changing it is cheap — but its consequences are invisible; nobody yet knows what it will do to the world.
By the time its consequences are CLEAR, the technology is woven into daily life — and changing it has become slow, expensive, and political.
This double bind is the Collingridge dilemma (David Collingridge, 1980): control is easiest exactly when knowledge is weakest.
It reframes EK IOC-1.A.3 as structural, not an excuse — the information needed to anticipate an effect often does not exist until adoption produces it.
slides.script: A deep dive for the enrichment track — beyond the AP exam, but it makes the whole unit sharper. David Collingridge noticed a cruel timing problem: early in a technology's life you could easily steer it, but you have no evidence about where it's going; once the evidence arrives, millions of people and dollars depend on it staying the course. That is why 'they should have known' is usually unfair, and why 'we'll fix it later' is usually expensive. Hold onto this for tomorrow — the responsible-programmer standard makes more sense once you see that the knowledge to anticipate effects is created by the very adoption that locks them in.

capture.q: Early in a technology's life, steering it is easy but ______.
capture.a: foreseeing where it leads is nearly hopeless — evidence hasn't arrived yet

capture.q: Once a technology's effects are obvious, ______.
capture.a: everyone already depends on it, so course-correction turns costly

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Two Verdicts, One Effect
slides.subheading: These map to LO IOC-1.A. Complete on your Guided Notes.
prompts: From the short-video app, name one effect a parent and a student would judge differently. State the effect ONCE, then give both verdicts.
Give an example of a computing advance boosting a non-computing field — medicine, engineering, communications, or the arts — and name what it made possible.
A news story says 'the app's designers should have known.' Using the idea of unanticipated effects, explain when that standard is unfair.
slides.directions: Answer in complete sentences. Then check yourself with the matching CFUs on the Topic 5.1 page.
slides.script: Stop and think — six minutes, full sentences on the Guided Notes. Prompt one enforces today's discipline: the effect must be stated once, singular, before the two verdicts are given — answers that list a good effect and a different bad effect miss the point of EK IOC-1.A.4. Prompt two checks the creativity-spillover idea with a concrete field and a concrete capability. Prompt three is the judgment call: hindsight makes every effect look predictable, but not every effect is anticipated in advance — strong answers name what information the designers could not have had. A few students will be called on to share.

slides.heading: End of Day 1
todayPoints: People create computing innovations, and daily tasks reorganize around them (EK IOC-1.A.1–A.2)
Computing advances fuel creativity in medicine, engineering, communications, and the arts (EK IOC-1.A.5)
Not every effect is anticipated in advance (EK IOC-1.A.3)
A single effect can be beneficial AND harmful — to different people, or the same person (EK IOC-1.A.4)
nextPoints: Day 2: innovations used in ways their creators never intended (LO IOC-1.B)
The CED's three named stories: the Web, targeted advertising, machine learning
Innovation Investigation #3 launches — you pick the innovation
slides.teaser: Before tomorrow: the World Wide Web was invented so physicists could swap research papers. Write down two things you did on the Web this week that its inventor never planned for.
slides.script: That's Day 1. Innovations are made by people, life reorders around them, the ripples reach medicine and the arts, surprises are guaranteed, and — the sentence to keep — a single effect can be viewed as both beneficial and harmful, by different people or the same person. Tomorrow the question flips from effects to intent: what happens when an innovation is used for things its creators never imagined, and what do responsible programmers owe the world when it is? Here's the teaser: the Web was built so physicists could swap papers — write down two things you did on it this week that its inventor never planned for. Guided Notes are collected at the end of class.

quiz.title: Topic 5.1 — Beneficial and Harmful Effects — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 8 questions, 15 minutes, no notes.

questions.stem: A city installs cameras that automatically ticket speeding drivers. Families near a school praise the effect — traffic slows down; commuters condemn the very same effect — their drive now takes longer. Per EK IOC-1.A.4, this situation illustrates:
options: An innovation with two unrelated effects — one beneficial and one harmful
An unanticipated effect that the city could not have predicted in advance
A single effect viewed as beneficial by some people and harmful by others
A harmful effect that cancels out the innovation's beneficial effects
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.A.4 states that a single effect can be viewed as both beneficial and harmful by different people, or even by the same person. Here there is exactly ONE effect — traffic slows — judged oppositely by families and commuters. A is the classic trap: it splits one effect into two separate ones, which is precisely what the EK rules out. B fails since slower traffic was the innovation's stated goal, fully anticipated. D invents a weighing contest the scenario never runs; the EK is about viewpoint, not about one effect outweighing another.

questions.stem: Per EK IOC-1.A.2, which statement best describes what typically happens when a new computing innovation becomes widely adopted?
options: The way people complete tasks often changes to incorporate the innovation
Older methods stay just as common — habits rarely shift for new tools
Every effect of the innovation is documented before adoption spreads
Task completion stays fixed while only entertainment habits change
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.A.2 is nearly verbatim in option A: the way people complete tasks often changes to incorporate new computing innovations — navigation, studying, and payment all reorganized around software. B contradicts the EK and observable history; old methods routinely fade. C contradicts EK IOC-1.A.3 — effects are not fully known in advance, let alone documented. D invents a distinction the CED never draws; task completion is exactly what changes.

questions.stem: A hospital rehearses surgeries on computer-generated 3D models of a patient's heart, and an animation studio's rendering technique is later adopted by architects. Per EK IOC-1.A.5, these examples best show:
options: Computing innovations replacing human experts in specialized fields
Harmful impacts of computing on the economy of traditional fields
The impossibility of anticipating how an innovation will be used
Advances in computing generating and increasing creativity in other fields
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.A.5 states that advances in computing have generated and increased creativity in other fields, such as medicine, engineering, communications, and the arts — surgery rehearsal and architecture are other fields gaining new creative capability. A misreads the scenarios: the surgeon and architect still do the work, with new tools. B assigns harm where the scenario describes expanded capability. C points at the wrong EK — anticipation limits are IOC-1.B.4's territory and are not what these examples demonstrate.

questions.stem: The World Wide Web was originally intended only for rapid and easy exchange of information within the scientific community. Today it hosts shopping, streaming, and social media. Per EK IOC-1.B.1, the Web's history best illustrates:
options: A harmful impact on society, the economy, and culture at once
A computing innovation used in ways its creators had not originally intended
An innovation that failed to achieve the purpose it was designed for
The risk of running a program with too few users to test it properly
questions.rationale: The Web is EK IOC-1.B.1's first named example: computing innovations can be used in ways that their creators had not originally intended — scientific information exchange grew into the platform of modern life. A mislabels the growth as harm; the EK presents it as unintended use, not damage. C is false: the Web achieved its scientific purpose AND far more. D inverts EK IOC-1.B.6 — the Web's story involves an enormous user base, not too small a one.

questions.stem: Per EK IOC-1.B.2, some ways computing innovations are used may have a harmful impact on which of the following? I. Society II. The economy III. Culture
options: I only
I and II only
II and III only
I, II, and III
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.B.2 names all three arenas explicitly: some of the ways computing innovations can be used may have a harmful impact on society, the economy, or culture. A reflects the misconception that harm must be broadly social to count. B drops culture, the arena students most often forget — yet cultural shifts (how people communicate, create, and relate) are named in the EK. C drops society, which the EK lists first. Only D matches the EK's full list.

questions.stem: Before releasing a photo-messaging app, its developers brainstorm unintended uses, add a reporting tool, and monitor early users. Months later the app is misused in a way no one on the team imagined. Per EK IOC-1.B.3 and IOC-1.B.4, which judgment is most accurate?
options: The team met its responsibility — considering every possible use of an innovation is not possible
The team was negligent — responsible programmers identify all misuses before launch
The team should have canceled the release until every unintended use was eliminated
The misuse shows that brainstorming unintended uses before launch has no real value
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.B.3 sets the standard — responsible programmers TRY to consider unintended ways their innovations can be used — and EK IOC-1.B.4 sets the limit: it is not possible for a programmer to consider all the ways an innovation can be used. The team did the trying (brainstorming, guardrails, monitoring), so the surprise misuse is not negligence. B demands the omniscience B.4 rules out. C makes release impossible for all software, for the same reason. D swings the other way — the impossibility of catching everything never cancels the duty to try.

questions.stem: A two-person team's free study app is featured in a viral video and gains 40 million users in a week. Its chat feature fills with spam rings, and moderation collapses. Per EK IOC-1.B.6, this best illustrates:
options: Targeted advertising misused at the individual and aggregate levels
Rapid sharing producing impacts beyond the intended purpose or the programmers' control
An anticipated effect the developers planned for from the start
Machine learning discovering information later used to discriminate
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.B.6 states that rapid sharing of a program, or running it with a large number of users, can result in significant impacts beyond the intended purpose or control of the programmer — a 40-million-user week that outruns a two-person team is the textbook case. A and D name other EK IOC-1.B.1 examples that do not appear in this scenario; there is no ad targeting and no pattern-mining here. C is contradicted by the collapse itself — a planned-for effect does not overwhelm its planners.

questions.stem: Image-recognition software built to sort vacation photos is later adapted by doctors to flag early-stage tumors — an application its developers never planned. Per EK IOC-1.B.5, this outcome is best described as:
options: A misuse that responsible programmers should have prevented before release
Evidence that the software failed to meet its original intended purpose
An unintended beneficial effect — the innovation led to advances in another field
A harmful impact on the culture of the medical profession
questions.rationale: EK IOC-1.B.5 states that computing innovations have often had unintended beneficial effects by leading to advances in other fields — photo-sorting software advancing medicine is exactly that pattern. A treats a benefit as a misuse; nothing here needed preventing. B is false: the software still sorts vacation photos, and a bonus use is not a failure. D assigns harm with no support in the scenario — earlier tumor detection is presented as an advance, and 'unintended' does not mean 'harmful.'

guide.title: Topic 5.1 — Beneficial and Harmful Effects — Teacher Guide
guide.subtitle: Big Idea 5: Impact of Computing · Topic 5.1 · 2 class periods + assessment block
overview: Topic 5.1 opens Big Idea 5 with the unit's central habit of mind: every computing innovation is created by people to serve a purpose, and every one of them produces effects its creators did not fully choose. Day 1 builds the effect-analysis toolkit — people create innovations, tasks reorganize around them, effects arrive unanticipated, and the exam's key structure: a single effect viewed as both beneficial and harmful, by different people or the same person. Day 2 turns to impact beyond intent: the CED's three named stories (the World Wide Web, targeted advertising, machine learning and data mining), harm at the scale of society, the economy, and culture, and the line between what responsible programmers must try to foresee and what nobody can.

This topic also hosts Innovation Investigation #3. Day 2's Stop and Think launches a Create-adjacent write-up in which each student documents one innovation's intended purpose, the data it consumes and produces, and its beneficial and harmful effects; the flex block provides the work time. The through-line starts with the Day 1 bell ringer: students list how one app helps and harms the same user — and discover the dual-view effect before it is named.
los: LO IOC-1.A — Explain how an effect of a computing innovation can be both beneficial and harmful.
LO IOC-1.B — Explain how a computing innovation can have an impact beyond its intended purpose.
eks: EK IOC-1.A.1 — People create computing innovations.
EK IOC-1.A.2 — The way people complete tasks often changes to incorporate new computing innovations.
EK IOC-1.A.3 — Not every effect of a computing innovation is anticipated in advance.
EK IOC-1.A.4 — A single effect can be viewed as both beneficial and harmful by different people, or even by the same person.
EK IOC-1.A.5 — Advances in computing have generated and increased creativity in other fields, such as medicine, engineering, communications, and the arts.

EK IOC-1.B.1 — Computing innovations can be used in ways that their creators had not originally intended: The World Wide Web was originally intended only for rapid and easy exchange of information within the scientific community. Targeted advertising is used to help businesses, but it can be misused at both individual and aggregate levels. Machine learning and data mining have enabled innovation in medicine, business, and science, but information discovered in this way has also been used to discriminate against groups of individuals.
EK IOC-1.B.2 — Some of the ways computing innovations can be used may have a harmful impact on society, the economy, or culture.
EK IOC-1.B.3 — Responsible programmers try to consider the unintended ways their computing innovations can be used and the potential beneficial and harmful effects of these new uses.
EK IOC-1.B.4 — It is not possible for a programmer to consider all the ways a computing innovation can be used.
EK IOC-1.B.5 — Computing innovations have often had unintended beneficial effects by leading to advances in other fields.
EK IOC-1.B.6 — Rapid sharing of a program or running a program with a large number of users can result in significant impacts beyond the intended purpose or control of the programmer.
materials: Printed Topic 5.1 quiz (student copies) for the assessment block.
Innovation Investigation #3 handout or notebook space — the write-up launches from Day 2's Stop and Think; Exercises 1–2 model the analysis.
prereqs: None within Big Idea 5 — this is its opening topic. Students should recall the 'computing innovation' definition from Topic 1.1 (a program is integral to its function); the Day 1 vocabulary slide reactivates it.

pacing.focus: Effects of innovations: created by people, felt everywhere, judged from two seats (LO IOC-1.A)

segments.segment: QOTD (BI5/T5.1/Q1) + bell ringer (3 helps and 3 harms for the same user)
How innovations change us: vocab, tasks, creativity spillover (EK IOC-1.A.1–A.2, A.5)
One effect, two verdicts + effect triage table (EK IOC-1.A.3–A.4)
Deep dive: the Collingridge dilemma (optional enrichment)
Day close + the Web teaser for Day 2

pacing.focus: Impact beyond intended purpose; responsibility and its limits; Investigation #3 launch (LO IOC-1.B)

segments.segment: QOTD (BI5/T5.1/Q2) + bell ringer (what the Web's inventor never planned)
Uses beyond intent: vocab, the CED's three named examples, scale (EK IOC-1.B.1–B.2, B.6)
Deep dive: GPS, missiles to meal delivery (optional enrichment)
Stop and Think — Innovation Investigation #3 launch

pacing.label: Flex / Assessment block — no new deck (uses the Topic Quiz)
pacing.focus: Not a numbered prep day. Run the Topic 5.1 quiz as the opener of the next class (doubles as spaced retrieval), then give Innovation Investigation #3 its work time: students turn the Day 2 Stop and Think draft into the Create-adjacent write-up — the innovation, its intended purpose, the data it consumes and produces, and its beneficial and harmful effects. The quiz and the investigation ship as handouts, so no new deck or notes are needed.

misconceptions.heading: “A good innovation has only good effects”
misconceptions.myth: Well-designed, genuinely useful technology produces positive effects; harm only comes from bad engineering or bad intentions.
misconceptions.reality: Effects depend on who is affected and how the innovation is used. Not every effect is anticipated in advance (EK IOC-1.A.3), and a single effect can be viewed as beneficial AND harmful by different people, or even the same person (EK IOC-1.A.4). The bell ringer's starred items make this personal before it is technical.

misconceptions.heading: “Responsible programmers catch every misuse”
misconceptions.myth: If a team is truly responsible, it finds every possible misuse before launch — any harm that slips through proves carelessness.
misconceptions.reality: The CED splits the standard in two: responsible programmers TRY to consider unintended uses and their effects (EK IOC-1.B.3), and it is NOT POSSIBLE to consider all the ways an innovation can be used (EK IOC-1.B.4). Exam options claiming a programmer 'considered every possible use' — or should have — are always wrong.

misconceptions.heading: “Harm means a person got hurt”
misconceptions.myth: A harmful impact requires an identifiable victim — someone injured, cheated, or directly damaged.
misconceptions.reality: EK IOC-1.B.2 names three arenas of harm beyond the individual: society, the economy, and culture. Job categories eliminated, groups discriminated against by mined data, cultural habits reshaped at scale — all count as harmful impacts on the exam even when no single named person is 'hurt.'

support: Give the sentence frame: 'One effect of ____ is ____. To ____, it is beneficial: ____. To ____, it is harmful: ____.' It physically forces the single-effect structure the exam rewards.
For Investigation #3, offer a menu of well-documented innovations (navigation apps, ride sharing, translation earbuds, fitness trackers) so research effort goes into effects, not topic hunting.
extension: Have students split targeted-advertising misuse into individual-level and aggregate-level cases and defend the distinction with concrete examples (EK IOC-1.B.1).
Deep-dive students apply the Collingridge dilemma to their Investigation #3 innovation: when could its harms have been cheaply steered, and what was actually knowable at that moment?

discussion: The Web's creator gave it away for free, expecting physics papers. How much of what the Web became is his responsibility — and would a world where inventors are liable for every use ever build anything?
Name an effect of a technology that you and an adult in your life judge in opposite ways. Whose judgment should count when schools or governments regulate it?
EK IOC-1.B.4 says no programmer can consider every use. Where should the line sit between 'reasonable effort to foresee misuse' and 'not the developer's problem anymore'?
guide.answerKeyNote: Each question's full rationale is on the KEY copy of the quiz (print it with the answer-key option). The short answer key below is auto-derived from the live quiz block, so it can never drift from the student questions.
